figures.caption: Figure 3: Diagram of FDTO spatial discretization procedure. (left) Node-stored variables (and precomputed grid metrics) are linearly interpolated to edge midpoints to obtain face states. (middle) Conservative numerical fluxes are evaluated on these faces using the interpolated states. (right) The nodal residual is assembled by a finite-difference flux divergence, i.e., differences of neighboring face fluxes in the computational ξ/η directions.
Figure 12: Representative airfoil geometries (NACA0012, NACA4424, RAE2822, and RAE5214).
Figure 11: Convergence comparison on lid-driven cavity flow at Re = 2500 on a 201 × 201 grid: relative L2 error versus epoch for FDTO, FDGN, and GenFVGN.

Figure 9: Comparisons of horizontal velocity along the vertical centerline (left) and vertical velocity along the horizontal centerline (right) for the time-averaged lid-driven cavity flows at Re = 2500 on a 201 × 201 grid.
Figure 10: Convergence comparison of FDTO and ODIL-SGR for lid-driven cavity flow on a 201 × 201 grid: relative L2 error versus runtime at Re = 2500, 3200, 5000, and 7500.

Figure 15: Comparison of surface pressure distributions: CFD, FDTO, and the parametric solver. Top-left: NACA0012(Re = 100, α = 15◦); top-right: NACA4424(Re = 200, α = 11◦); bottom-left: RAE2822(Re = 500, α = 7◦); bottom-right: RAE5214(Re = 1000, α = 3◦).
Figure 22: Effect of time-stepping choice (∆t=0.1, 0.05, 0.01) on lid-driven cavity (Re = 2500), and NACA0012 (Re = 100, α = 15◦).
Figure 21: Comparison of inner iterations on convergence across four cases: diffusion equation, flow mixing problem, lid-driven cavity (Re = 2500) and NACA0012 (Re = 100, α = 15◦).

Figure 14: Comparison of airfoil flow fields and absolute error maps.For each case, the columns (left to right) show the reference CFD solution, FDTO prediction, and the parametric solver prediction. The rows (top to bottom) report the velocity-magnitude contours, the absolute velocity error, the pressure contours, and the absolute pressure error.
Figure 4: Schematic of N-C-N averaging operator on body-fitted meshes.
Figure 5: Boundary Conditions for the lid-driven cavity (Uniform).
Figure 1: Paradigms of PDE solvers. top: CFD; middle: FDTO; bottom: PINN.
Figure 24: Grid-resolution study for lid-driven cavity flow at Re = 2500: relative L2 error versus epoch on 151 × 151, 201 × 201, 251 × 251, and 301 × 301 grids.
Figure 25: Effect of TS optimization on lid-driven cavity flow at Re = 2500 on a 201 × 201 grid: velocity-magnitude contours (top) and absolute error maps (bottom) for FDTO with and without TS.
Figure 23: Effect of optimizer choice (Adam, L-BFGS, and Soap) across four cases: diffusion equation, flow mixing problem, lid-driven cavity (Re = 2500), and NACA0012 (Re = 100, α = 15◦).
Table 5: Relative L2 errors of |u| and p for airfoil cases: FDTO versus the parametric solver.
Table 6: Comparison of lift and drag coefficients (Cl, Cd) for airfoil cases: CFD, FDTO, and the parametric solver.
Figure 13: Computational domain, boundary conditions (left), and body-fitted structured mesh (right) for airfoil flows (curvelinear).
Figure 8: FDTO-optimized lid-driven cavity streamlines at Re = 10000 on a 201 × 201 grid for different time steps (Nstep = 1000, 1400, 2000).
Figure 6: Comparison of CFD, FDTO, and ODIL-SGR for the lid-driven cavity at Re = 2500 (left) and Re = 5000 (right), showing velocity fields and absolute error maps on a 201 × 201 grid.
Figure 7: FDTO-optimized streamlines for lid-driven cavity flow from Re = 2500 to Re = 7500 on a 201 × 201 grid.

Figure 27: Ablation study of the N-C-N treatment for an airfoil case: comparison of velocity magnitude (left), pressure (middle), and absolute pressure error (right) without (top row) and with (bottom row) N-C-N.
Figure 26: Effect of time-stepping optimization on lid-driven cavity flow at Re = 2500 on a 201 × 201 grid: convergence of relative L2 error with and without TS.
Figure 2: An example of coordinate transformation from physical space (left) to computational space (right).
Figure 19: Comparison of flow field (left) and absolute error (right) for flow mixing problem on a 256 × 256 grid.
Figure 18: Comparison of flow field (left) and absolute error (right) for diffusion equation on a 101 × 101 grid.
Figure 20: Comparison of FDTO and an explicit time-marching finite-volume solver (ETM-FVM) on a 201 × 201 lid-driven cavity grid: relative L2 error versus epoch at Re = 2500 and Re = 3200.
